name: Engineering, Procurement, and Construction (EPC) Contract
summary: Drafts comprehensive Engineering, Procurement, and Construction (EPC) contracts as regulatory documents for turnkey industrial facility projects. Establishes clear rights, obligations, risk allocations, and technical requirements between project owners and contractors. Use this skill for energy law-compliant EPC agreements in infrastructure developments.
content:
# Enhanced EPC Contract Drafting Workflow

You are tasked with drafting a comprehensive Engineering, Procurement, and Construction (EPC) Contract that serves as a legally binding regulatory document governing a turnkey construction project. This contract must establish clear rights, obligations, and risk allocations between the project owner and the EPC contractor for the complete design, procurement, construction, testing, and commissioning of an industrial facility or infrastructure project.

## Initial Information Gathering and Document Research

Before beginning the drafting process, conduct a thorough review of all available project documentation and reference materials. Search through any uploaded documents to identify critical project-specific information including the project description and technical specifications, the identities and corporate details of the contracting parties, preliminary scope definitions or requests for proposal, budget parameters and pricing expectations, and any existing term sheets or letters of intent that may inform the contract terms. Extract specific details such as party names, addresses, project locations, capacity specifications, budget figures, and timeline expectations that appear in the source materials.

If the user has provided sample EPC contracts, precedent agreements, or template documents, analyze these materials to understand the preferred contract structure, standard provisions, and any organization-specific language or requirements. Pay particular attention to any jurisdiction-specific requirements, industry standards applicable to the project type, and risk allocation preferences reflected in the reference materials. When you identify gaps in the available information, note these deficiencies so you can request clarification from the user before finalizing critical contract provisions.

## Contract Structure and Comprehensive Drafting

Draft the EPC Contract following a logical structure that progresses from fundamental definitions and scope provisions through detailed commercial terms, technical requirements, risk allocation mechanisms, and administrative provisions. Begin with a detailed recitals section that establishes the context and purpose of the agreement, describing the owner's objectives in developing the facility, the contractor's qualifications and experience in delivering similar projects, and the parties' intent to enter into a turnkey arrangement where the contractor assumes comprehensive responsibility for engineering, procurement, and construction.

### Parties and Definitional Framework

Identify each contracting party with complete legal precision, including the full legal name exactly as it appears in formation documents, the type of legal entity and jurisdiction of organization, the principal place of business and registered office address, tax identification numbers where appropriate for the jurisdiction, and the specific authorized representatives who will execute the agreement along with their corporate titles. For the EPC contractor, address whether the contractor is a single entity, a joint venture, or a consortium, and if multiple entities are involved, clearly delineate the liability structure, the lead contractor designation, and whether joint and several liability applies or whether liability is allocated among consortium members.

Establish a comprehensive definitions section that provides precise meanings for all technical and commercial terms used throughout the agreement. Define critical concepts such as "Substantial Completion" with specific objective criteria including completion of all work except minor punch list items not affecting facility operation, successful completion of all mechanical and electrical testing, achievement of specified performance parameters during initial testing, and readiness for the owner to assume beneficial occupancy. Define "Final Completion" as the milestone when all punch list items have been completed, all performance tests have been successfully passed with guaranteed performance levels achieved, all operating and maintenance documentation has been delivered, all training has been provided, and any required reliability demonstration period has been successfully completed.

Include definitions for "Contract Price," "Change Order," "Force Majeure," "Defect," "Warranty Period," and other terms that will govern the parties' rights and obligations. Ensure that technical terms specific to the project type are defined with reference to industry standards, engineering specifications, or regulatory requirements applicable to the facility being constructed.

### Scope of Work and Technical Specifications

Provide an exhaustive description of the contractor's scope of work that leaves no ambiguity about what is included in the turnkey delivery obligation. Begin with a narrative overview of the complete facility or infrastructure being delivered, including the facility type such as a power generation plant, petrochemical facility, water treatment plant, or other industrial installation, the design capacity and throughput specifications, the intended operational purpose and performance objectives, and the overall project delivery philosophy emphasizing the turnkey nature of the contractor's obligations.

Detail the engineering responsibilities encompassing all design disciplines required for the complete facility. Specify that the contractor shall perform all conceptual engineering, basic engineering, and detailed engineering necessary to define the facility and enable procurement and construction. Address civil and structural engineering including site development, foundations, buildings, and structural steel; mechanical engineering covering all rotating and static equipment, piping systems, and material handling systems; electrical engineering including power distribution, motor control, lighting, and grounding systems; instrumentation and control engineering encompassing all measurement devices, control systems, distributed control systems or programmable logic controllers, and human-machine interfaces; and any specialized engineering disciplines such as process engineering, environmental engineering, or safety systems engineering specific to the facility type.

Describe the procurement obligations in comprehensive detail, establishing that the contractor shall procure, expedite, inspect, and deliver to the site all equipment, materials, components, and consumables necessary for the complete facility. Specify major equipment packages that must be provided, such as turbines, generators, compressors, reactors, vessels, heat exchangers, pumps, or other primary process equipment. Address bulk materials including structural steel, piping and valves, electrical cable and components, instrumentation and control devices, insulation, painting, and fireproofing materials. Clarify the contractor's responsibility for spare parts, special tools, and maintenance equipment necessary for facility operation. Identify any owner-furnished equipment or materials that are excluded from the contractor's procurement scope, and establish clear interface requirements and delivery coordination obligations for such owner-furnished items.

Elaborate on the construction and installation responsibilities, requiring the contractor to perform all site preparation, earthwork, and civil construction; install all foundations, structures, and buildings; erect and install all mechanical equipment, piping systems, and material handling systems; install all electrical and instrumentation systems; complete all insulation, painting, and fireproofing work; and execute all other construction activities necessary to achieve a complete and operational facility. Specify quality standards and workmanship requirements, referencing applicable codes, standards, and industry best practices such as ASME codes for pressure vessels and piping, IEEE standards for electrical systems, ISA standards for instrumentation, and any project-specific quality requirements.

Address the contractor's testing and commissioning obligations in detail, requiring factory acceptance testing of major equipment prior to shipment, pre-commissioning activities including flushing, cleaning, and functional testing of individual systems, integrated systems commissioning to verify proper interaction of all facility systems, performance testing to demonstrate achievement of guaranteed performance parameters, and any required reliability demonstration runs or sustained operation periods. Specify the owner's right to witness testing, the documentation requirements for test results, and the acceptance criteria that must be satisfied.

Include the contractor's obligations for regulatory compliance and permitting, specifying whether the contractor or owner will be responsible for obtaining environmental permits, construction permits, operating licenses, and other regulatory approvals. Address the contractor's responsibility to design and construct the facility in compliance with all applicable laws, regulations, codes, and standards. Establish the contractor's training obligations, requiring the contractor to provide comprehensive operations training and maintenance training for the owner's personnel, including classroom instruction, hands-on training, and the provision of training materials and documentation.

Detail the documentation deliverables the contractor must provide, including design basis documents, engineering calculations, equipment specifications and data sheets, vendor documentation, construction drawings, as-built drawings reflecting all field changes, operations and maintenance manuals, spare parts lists and recommended inventory levels, and warranty documentation for all equipment and systems. Specify the format, number of copies, and delivery schedule for all documentation.

Clearly delineate scope exclusions and owner responsibilities to avoid disputes about the boundaries of the contractor's obligations. Identify items such as land acquisition, site access roads, utility connections from public infrastructure to the site boundary, owner's personnel costs, financing costs, insurance premiums for owner-controlled policies, permits and fees that are the owner's responsibility, and any other items explicitly excluded from the contract price. Describe interface requirements with other contractors if the owner has separately contracted for certain work, and establish coordination obligations and responsibility for interface management.

### Commercial Terms and Payment Structure

Establish the total contract price with absolute clarity, stating the exact amount in the applicable currency and specifying whether this is a lump-sum turnkey price, a guaranteed maximum price with cost reimbursement up to the cap, a cost-plus arrangement with defined reimbursable costs and fee structure, or a hybrid approach combining different pricing mechanisms for different work elements. For a lump-sum turnkey price, confirm that the price is fixed and includes all engineering, procurement, construction, testing, commissioning, training, documentation, and other services necessary to achieve final completion, subject only to adjustments for owner-directed changes, differing site conditions, force majeure events, or other specified change events.

Detail the payment schedule tied to objective milestones and deliverables. Structure the payment schedule to align with the contractor's anticipated cash flow requirements while protecting the owner's interests through retention of sufficient funds to ensure completion. A typical structure might include an advance payment upon contract execution representing five to ten percent of the contract price, subject to the contractor providing an advance payment guarantee; progress payments at defined engineering milestones such as completion of basic engineering design and completion of detailed engineering, each representing a specified percentage of the contract price; progress payments upon delivery of major equipment to the site, calculated as a percentage of the delivered equipment value; monthly progress payments during construction based on the percentage of construction work completed, determined through joint measurement and verification; a substantial payment upon achievement of substantial completion after deducting all retention amounts; and a final payment upon achievement of final completion and satisfaction of all performance guarantees, releasing all retained amounts.

Specify the retention or holdback mechanism, typically requiring the owner to retain five to ten percent of each progress payment until substantial completion, at which point a portion of the retention may be released, with the balance retained until final completion and expiration of warranty periods. Address whether the contractor may substitute retention with a retention bond or letter of credit to improve cash flow.

Establish the invoicing and payment process, requiring the contractor to submit detailed invoices within a specified number of days after achieving each milestone, supported by documentation evidencing milestone completion such as engineering deliverables, equipment delivery receipts, construction progress reports, or test results. Provide the owner with a defined review period, typically fifteen to thirty days, to review and approve invoices, with payment due within a specified period after approval, typically thirty days. Include provisions for dispute resolution if the owner disputes an invoice, allowing the owner to pay undisputed amounts while the parties resolve disputed items, and establishing interest charges for late payment of undisputed amounts.

Address price adjustment mechanisms if the contract includes provisions for escalation or de-escalation based on changes in labor costs, material costs, or currency exchange rates. Specify the indices that will be used for adjustment calculations, the baseline values, the adjustment formula, and any caps on total price adjustments. Clarify the treatment of taxes, duties, and governmental charges, specifying which party bears responsibility for sales taxes, value-added taxes, customs duties, import taxes, and other governmental impositions, and whether such amounts are included in the contract price or will be reimbursed separately.

### Project Schedule and Time Management

Establish a detailed project schedule with clearly defined milestones, durations, and completion dates. Specify the contract commencement date or identify the conditions precedent that must be satisfied before time begins to run, such as issuance of a notice to proceed by the owner, provision of site access to the contractor, receipt of necessary governmental approvals or permits, or satisfaction of financing conditions. State the overall contract duration from commencement to final completion, expressed in calendar days or months.

Identify all critical milestone dates including completion of basic engineering design, completion of detailed engineering and issuance of all construction drawings, award of major equipment purchase orders, delivery of long-lead equipment items to the site, commencement of site construction activities, achievement of mechanical completion when all equipment and systems are installed, commencement of commissioning activities, achievement of substantial completion, completion of performance testing, and achievement of final completion. For each milestone, specify the target date and the criteria that must be satisfied to achieve the milestone.

Provide detailed definitions of substantial completion and final completion that establish objective, verifiable criteria. Substantial completion should be defined to occur when the facility is sufficiently complete that the owner can assume beneficial occupancy and begin commercial operation, even if minor punch list items remain. Specific criteria might include completion of all work except minor punch list items that do not prevent safe operation or materially impair the facility's functionality, successful completion of all mechanical and electrical testing demonstrating that all systems operate as designed, achievement of specified performance parameters during initial performance testing, receipt of all regulatory approvals necessary for commercial operation, and delivery of all operations and maintenance documentation necessary for the owner to operate the facility.

Final completion should be defined to occur when absolutely all work has been completed without exception, all punch list items have been closed, all performance tests have been successfully passed with the facility demonstrating sustained achievement of all guaranteed performance parameters, all training has been provided, all documentation has been delivered in final form, and any required reliability demonstration period has been successfully completed. Specify the process for certifying achievement of substantial completion and final completion, including the contractor's obligation to notify the owner when the contractor believes a milestone has been achieved, the owner's right to inspect and verify completion, the issuance of a completion certificate by the owner or an independent engineer, and the consequences of achieving each completion milestone such as commencement of warranty periods or release of retention amounts.

Establish a comprehensive framework for managing schedule delays and granting time extensions. Identify the categories of events that will entitle the contractor to an extension of time, distinguishing between excusable delays that grant time relief without additional compensation and compensable delays that grant both time relief and cost recovery. Excusable delays typically include force majeure events such as natural disasters, wars, epidemics, or other events beyond either party's control; changes in law or regulations that affect the project; and delays caused by governmental authorities in issuing required permits or approvals. Compensable delays typically include owner-caused delays such as late provision of owner-furnished equipment, delayed access to the site, or interference with the contractor's work; changes in scope directed by the owner; and differing site conditions that differ materially from the conditions represented in the contract documents.

Specify the notice requirements and documentation the contractor must provide to claim schedule relief, including prompt written notice of the delay event, detailed description of the cause and anticipated impact, contemporaneous documentation of the delay's effect on the critical path schedule, and a request for specific time extension. Establish the owner's right to review and approve or dispute time extension requests, and provide a mechanism for resolving disputes over entitlement to time extensions or the appropriate duration of extensions.

Address the consequences of the contractor's failure to achieve substantial completion or final completion by the specified dates. Establish a liquidated damages regime that provides the owner with predetermined compensation for delay damages without requiring proof of actual damages. Specify the daily or weekly liquidated damage amount for late substantial completion, which should represent a reasonable pre-estimate of the owner's actual damages from delay such as lost revenue, extended financing costs, or continued reliance on existing facilities. Similarly, specify liquidated damages for late final completion if final completion is separately incentivized. Establish any cap on total liquidated damages, typically ranging from five to fifteen percent of the contract price, and clarify whether liquidated damages are the owner's exclusive remedy for delay or whether the owner retains the right to terminate the contract for extended delays.

Include provisions for early completion bonuses if the owner wishes to incentivize ahead-of-schedule delivery, specifying the bonus amount for achieving substantial completion ahead of schedule and any conditions that must be satisfied to earn the bonus.

### Performance Guarantees and Testing Protocols

Establish detailed, measurable performance guarantees that define the technical and operational standards the completed facility must achieve. The specific guarantees will depend on the facility type, but commonly include output capacity guarantees specifying the facility's production capacity under defined operating conditions, efficiency guarantees such as heat rate for power plants or yield for processing facilities, environmental performance guarantees for emissions of regulated pollutants, reliability and availability guarantees, and consumption guarantees for utilities or consumables. For each performance parameter, state the guaranteed value with precision, including units of measurement and any tolerances.

Specify the test conditions and reference conditions under which performance will be measured and guaranteed. Address whether guarantees apply at ISO standard conditions, site-specific ambient conditions, or other defined reference conditions. Identify all relevant operating parameters that affect performance such as ambient temperature, barometric pressure, relative humidity, fuel specifications including heating value and composition, feedstock quality specifications, and cooling water temperature. Establish correction curves or formulas that will be used to adjust measured performance to the guaranteed reference conditions.

Detail the performance testing protocol that will govern verification of guaranteed performance. Specify when performance testing will occur relative to substantial completion, typically requiring performance testing to commence within a defined period after substantial completion is achieved. Establish the duration of performance testing, which may range from a few days for simple tests to several weeks for complex facilities requiring multiple test runs under varying conditions. Specify the number of valid test runs required, the criteria for a valid test run including minimum duration, stable operating conditions, and absence of equipment malfunctions, and the method for calculating guaranteed performance from multiple test runs such as averaging all valid runs or taking the best result.

Address the role of independent testing engineers or third-party witnesses if their participation is required. Specify the testing procedures and protocols that will be followed, referencing industry standards such as ASME Performance Test Codes where applicable. Establish the instrumentation and measurement requirements, including the accuracy and calibration requirements for all test instruments, the location of measurement points, and the data recording and validation procedures. Detail the documentation requirements for performance testing, including test plans that must be submitted for owner approval prior to testing, real-time data recording during tests, and comprehensive test reports presenting all data, calculations, and conclusions.

Provide for retesting opportunities if initial performance tests do not demonstrate achievement of guaranteed performance. Allow the contractor a reasonable period to optimize the facility, make adjustments, repair deficiencies, or replace underperforming equipment, and then conduct additional performance tests. Specify any limitations on the number of retests permitted and any costs associated with retesting.

Establish the consequences of failing to achieve guaranteed performance levels. Implement a liquidated damages regime for performance deficiencies that correlates the magnitude of the shortfall to the liquidated damage amount. For capacity shortfalls, a typical formula might assess liquidated damages equal to a specified dollar amount per unit of capacity shortfall, such as dollars per kilowatt of capacity below the guarantee. For efficiency shortfalls, liquidated damages might be calculated based on the present value of increased operating costs over the facility's expected operating life resulting from the efficiency degradation. For environmental performance failures, liquidated damages might reflect the cost of additional emissions control equipment or the economic impact of operating restrictions.

Specify any caps on performance liquidated damages, and clarify whether performance liquidated damages are the owner's exclusive remedy for performance deficiencies or whether the owner retains additional rights. Address the owner's right to reject the facility if performance deficiencies are so substantial that the facility fails to meet its intended purpose, establishing threshold levels of performance shortfall that would trigger rejection rights. Clarify the interaction between performance guarantees and warranty obligations, specifying whether performance deficiencies that manifest after final completion will be addressed through warranty claims or additional liquidated damages.

Consider including performance bonus provisions that reward the contractor for exceeding guaranteed performance levels, creating a balanced incentive structure that encourages optimization. Specify the bonus amount for each unit of performance improvement above the guarantee and any cap on total performance bonuses.

### Risk Allocation and Change Management

Establish a structured change order process that provides a clear mechanism for managing changes to the contract scope, schedule, or price while maintaining proper documentation and authorization. Define the circumstances that constitute a change, including owner-requested modifications to the scope of work, design changes required to comply with changes in applicable laws or regulations, modifications necessary to address differing site conditions, changes required to incorporate technological improvements or address obsolescence, and constructive changes resulting from owner actions, decisions, or interpretations that materially affect the contractor's work.

Require that all changes be documented through formal written change orders executed by authorized representatives of both parties before the changed work is performed, except in emergency situations where immediate action is necessary to protect life, safety, or property, or to prevent damage to the work. For emergency changes, require the contractor to notify the owner immediately and obtain retroactive change order approval as soon as practicable.

Detail the change order initiation and evaluation process. Require any party proposing a change to submit a written change request describing the proposed modification, the reason for the change, and the anticipated impact. Upon receipt of a change request, require the contractor to prepare and submit a detailed change order proposal within a specified timeframe, typically ten to twenty business days. The change order proposal must include a comprehensive description of the changed work, a detailed breakdown of all costs associated with the change including direct labor costs, materials and equipment costs, subcontractor charges, and indirect costs, the contractor's markup for overhead and profit calculated according to the contract's pricing methodology, the impact on the project schedule with any requested time extension and supporting critical path analysis, the effect on performance guarantees or other technical commitments, and any other impacts on the contractor's obligations or the owner's interests.

Establish the pricing methodology for change orders, which may include lump-sum pricing for discrete, well-defined changes where the scope and cost can be determined with certainty; unit price pricing for changes involving quantifiable units of work, using unit prices established in the contract or negotiated for the specific change; time-and-materials pricing for changes where the scope is uncertain, using predetermined labor rates for each craft or classification, actual material costs plus a specified markup, and equipment rental rates; or cost-plus pricing where the contractor is reimbursed for actual costs plus a percentage fee or fixed fee for overhead and profit. Specify maximum allowable markups for contractor overhead and profit on change order work, typically ranging from ten to twenty percent depending on the nature of the work and whether it is self-performed or subcontracted.

Provide the owner with a defined period to review and respond to change order proposals, typically fifteen to thirty days. Grant the owner the right to request additional information, alternative approaches, or revised pricing. Establish a negotiation process for resolving disagreements over change order pricing, entitlement, or schedule impact. Address how the parties will handle situations where they agree that a change is necessary but disagree on pricing, potentially requiring the contractor to proceed with the work under a directed change order while pricing disputes are resolved through the contract's dispute resolution mechanism, with the contractor entitled to recover its reasonable costs plus a reasonable profit as ultimately determined.

Implement cumulative change order tracking requirements, requiring the contractor to maintain records of all change orders and their cumulative impact on the contract price and schedule. Establish thresholds that, if exceeded, may trigger additional rights such as the owner's right to terminate the contract for convenience if cumulative changes exceed a specified percentage of the original contract price, or the contractor's right to request contract renegotiation or termination if cumulative changes fundamentally alter the nature of the project.

Address the treatment of disputed changes where the parties disagree on whether a change has occurred or which party should bear the cost. Require the contractor to provide prompt written notice of any condition or event that the contractor believes constitutes a compensable change, including detailed documentation of the basis for the claim. Establish the owner's right to dispute the contractor's entitlement while requiring the contractor to continue performance, with the dispute to be resolved through the contract's dispute resolution process.

### Indemnification, Liability, and Insurance

Establish comprehensive indemnification obligations that allocate responsibility for third-party claims and losses. Require the contractor to indemnify, defend, and hold harmless the owner, its affiliates, parent companies, subsidiaries, and their respective officers, directors, employees, agents, and representatives from and against all claims, demands, suits, actions, proceedings, losses, damages, liabilities, costs, and expenses including reasonable attorneys' fees and court costs arising out of, resulting from, or related to the contractor's performance of the work under the contract. Specify the categories of indemnified claims including bodily injury, illness, or death of any person including the contractor's employees, subcontractors, or third parties; damage to or destruction of property including the work itself, the owner's property, or third-party property; violations of law, regulation, or permit requirements; infringement of patents, copyrights, trademarks, or other intellectual property rights; environmental contamination or releases of hazardous materials; and breach of the contractor's obligations under the contract.

Establish appropriate limitations on the contractor's indemnification obligations to ensure enforceability and fairness. Exclude from the contractor's indemnity obligation any claims arising solely from the owner's negligence, willful misconduct, or breach of contract, unless such limitation would be unenforceable under applicable law. Address the treatment of claims arising from the joint negligence of both parties, specifying whether indemnification will be allocated based on comparative fault or whether each party will bear its own defense costs and liability.

Provide for reciprocal indemnification by the owner for claims arising from the owner's negligence, willful misconduct, or breach of contract, or from defects in owner-furnished equipment, materials, or information. Establish procedural requirements for indemnification claims including the obligation of the indemnified party to provide prompt written notice of any claim to the indemnifying party, the right of the indemnifying party to assume control of the defense and settlement of the claim using counsel of its choice, the obligation of the indemnified party to cooperate in the defense and provide reasonable assistance, and any limitations on the indemnifying party's settlement authority such as requiring the indemnified party's consent for settlements that impose obligations on the indemnified party beyond monetary payments.

Detail the insurance requirements that the contractor must maintain throughout the project and for specified periods thereafter. Require commercial general liability insurance on an occurrence basis with minimum limits adequate for the project's risk profile, typically ranging from ten million to fifty million dollars per occurrence and in the aggregate, covering bodily injury, property damage, personal injury, and advertising injury. Require workers' compensation insurance as required by law in all jurisdictions where work will be performed, and employers' liability insurance with minimum limits typically of one million dollars per accident, one million dollars per employee for disease, and one million dollars aggregate for disease. Require automobile liability insurance covering all owned, hired, and non-owned vehicles used in connection with the work, with combined single limits typically of at least one million dollars.

Require professional liability insurance covering errors, omissions, and negligent acts in the performance of engineering and design services, with minimum limits typically ranging from five million to twenty-five million dollars per claim and in the aggregate. Specify whether professional liability insurance must be maintained on an occurrence basis or whether claims-made coverage is acceptable, and if claims-made coverage is permitted, require the contractor to maintain coverage or purchase extended reporting period coverage for a specified period after project completion, typically three to five years.

Require builder's risk or installation floater insurance on an all-risk basis covering the full replacement value of the work and all materials and equipment incorporated into the work or stored at the site. Specify whether this insurance will be maintained by the contractor or the owner, and if maintained by the owner, address the contractor's obligation to cooperate in claims and the allocation of deductibles. Address additional insurance requirements relevant to the specific project such as marine cargo insurance for international equipment shipments, covering all risks of physical loss or damage from the point of origin to final delivery at the project site; pollution liability insurance if the project involves environmental risks, covering sudden and gradual pollution conditions; and umbrella or excess liability insurance providing additional limits above the primary liability policies.

Specify that all liability insurance policies must name the owner and its affiliates as additional insureds with respect to the contractor's operations under the contract, include blanket waivers of subrogation in favor of the owner and its affiliates, provide that the contractor's insurance is primary and non-contributory with respect to any insurance maintained by the owner, and require the insurer to provide at least thirty days' advance written notice to the owner of any cancellation, non-renewal, or material modification of coverage. Require the contractor to provide certificates of insurance evidencing all required coverage prior to commencing any work, and to maintain such coverage continuously until all work has been completed and all warranty obligations have been satisfied.

Establish liability caps and exclusions that allocate risk appropriately between the parties. Consider limiting each party's liability for consequential, indirect, incidental, or punitive damages, excluding such damages except in cases of gross negligence, willful misconduct, or breach of confidentiality obligations, or for specific obligations such as indemnification, intellectual property infringement, or environmental liabilities where consequential damages limitations may be inappropriate. Consider establishing a cap on the contractor's total aggregate liability under the contract, typically equal to the contract price or a multiple thereof, subject to exceptions for uncapped liabilities such as indemnification obligations, intellectual property infringement, willful misconduct, or fraud.

### Warranties and Defect Correction

Establish comprehensive warranty obligations requiring the contractor to warrant that all work will be free from defects in materials, workmanship, and design, and will conform to the contract requirements including all specifications, drawings, and performance guarantees. Specify the warranty period, which typically commences upon substantial completion or final completion and extends for a defined duration, commonly twelve to twenty-four months for general construction work, with potentially longer warranty periods for specific equipment or systems. Address whether warranty periods will be extended for portions of the work that are repaired or replaced during the warranty period, typically requiring the warranty period for repaired or replaced items to restart from the date of repair or replacement.

Require the contractor to warrant that all equipment and materials incorporated into the work are new unless otherwise specified, are suitable for their intended purpose, and are free from defects in design, materials, and workmanship. Require the contractor to assign or pass through to the owner all manufacturers' and suppliers' warranties for equipment and materials, and to assist the owner in enforcing such warranties. Specify that the contractor's warranty obligations are in addition to, and not in lieu of, any manufacturers' or suppliers' warranties.

Detail the owner's rights and the contractor's obligations upon discovery of defects during the warranty period. Require the owner to provide prompt written notice to the contractor of any defects discovered, describing the nature and location of the defect. Require the contractor to commence correction of warranted defects within a specified period after receiving notice, typically ranging from twenty-four hours for urgent defects affecting safety or operations to ten business days for non-urgent defects. Require the contractor to complete correction of defects within a reasonable time, and to perform all corrective work in a manner that minimizes disruption to the owner's operations.

Specify that the contractor's defect correction obligations include all costs associated with correction including labor, materials, equipment, engineering, transportation, and any consequential work necessary to access and repair the defect and restore the work to its original condition. Address the owner's rights if the contractor fails to timely correct warranted defects, including the owner's right to correct the defects itself or through third parties and to charge the contractor for all costs incurred, with such costs recoverable from any retention amounts, warranty security, or through legal action.

Establish warranty security requirements, potentially requiring the contractor to provide a warranty bond, letter of credit, or other security in an amount equal to a percentage of the contract price, typically five to ten percent, to secure the contractor's warranty obligations. Specify the conditions for release of warranty security, typically upon expiration of all warranty periods and satisfaction of all warranty obligations.

### Termination Rights and Consequences

Establish the circumstances under which either party may terminate the contract and the consequences of termination. Provide the owner with the right to terminate the contract for cause if the contractor commits material breaches such as abandoning the work, failing to prosecute the work with sufficient labor and equipment to meet the schedule, failing to correct defective work after notice, or becoming insolvent or filing for bankruptcy protection. Require the owner to provide written notice of default specifying the nature of the breach and providing the contractor with a cure period, typically ten to thirty days, to remedy the breach. If the contractor fails to cure within the specified period, authorize the owner to terminate the contract for cause.

Upon termination for cause, establish the owner's rights to take possession of the work and all materials, equipment, and contractor documents at the site, to complete the work itself or through other contractors, and to recover from the contractor all costs incurred to complete the work in excess of the unpaid balance of the contract price. Provide that the contractor remains liable for all damages resulting from the contractor's default including delay damages, increased completion costs, and any other consequential damages not otherwise excluded by the contract.

Provide the owner with the right to terminate the contract for convenience at any time for any reason or no reason upon providing written notice to the contractor. Upon termination for convenience, require the contractor to immediately cease work except as directed by the owner to protect completed work or to facilitate transition to a replacement contractor, demobilize from the site in an orderly manner, assign to the owner all subcontracts and purchase orders as directed by the owner, and deliver to the owner all completed and partially completed work, materials, equipment, and contractor documents.

Establish the contractor's compensation rights upon termination for convenience, entitling the contractor to payment for all work performed prior to the termination date, all materials and equipment procured or committed for the project, all demobilization costs, and a reasonable allowance for overhead and profit on work performed but not for anticipated profit on unperformed work. Specify the process for determining the termination payment, typically requiring the contractor to submit a detailed termination claim within a specified period, subject to the owner's audit and verification.

Provide the contractor with limited termination rights for owner defaults such as failure to make undisputed payments when due, suspension of the work for an extended period for reasons not attributable to the contractor, or material breach of the owner's obligations. Require the contractor to provide written notice of the owner's default and a cure period before exercising termination rights. Upon termination for owner default, entitle the contractor to payment for all work performed, all costs incurred, and anticipated profit on the entire contract.

Address the treatment of termination in force majeure situations where performance becomes impossible or commercially impracticable due to events beyond either party's control. Provide that if a force majeure event continues for an extended period, typically exceeding six months, either party may terminate the contract without liability, with the contractor entitled to payment for work performed and costs incurred but not for anticipated profit on unperformed work.

### Dispute Resolution and Governing Law

Establish a multi-tiered dispute resolution process designed to resolve disputes efficiently while preserving the parties' relationship and maintaining project momentum. Require that all disputes, claims, or controversies arising out of or relating to the contract first be addressed through good faith negotiations between senior executives of both parties who have authority to settle the dispute. Specify a negotiation period, typically thirty days, during which the parties will attempt to resolve the dispute through direct discussions.

If negotiation fails to resolve the dispute, require the parties to submit the dispute to mediation before a mutually acceptable mediator or a mediator appointed by a designated mediation organization. Specify the mediation procedures, location, and cost allocation. Require the parties to participate in mediation in good faith, but clarify that neither party is obligated to settle the dispute through mediation.

If mediation fails to resolve the dispute within a specified period, typically sixty days from initiation of mediation, authorize either party to pursue binding dispute resolution through either litigation or arbitration as specified in the contract. If arbitration is selected, specify the arbitration rules that will govern such as the International Chamber of Commerce Rules, the American Arbitration Association Commercial Arbitration Rules, or other recognized arbitration rules. Specify the number of arbitrators, typically one or three depending on the dispute value, the method for selecting arbitrators, the seat of arbitration, and the language of arbitration proceedings. Establish that arbitration awards will be final and binding and may be enforced in any court of competent jurisdiction.

If litigation is selected, specify the exclusive jurisdiction and venue for any legal proceedings, identifying the specific courts that will have jurisdiction such as the state and federal courts located in a particular jurisdiction. Include a consent to jurisdiction and venue in the specified courts and a waiver of any objection to jurisdiction or venue.

Establish the governing law that will apply to interpret and enforce the contract, specifying the law of a particular jurisdiction without regard to its conflicts of law principles. Consider whether the United Nations Convention on Contracts for the International Sale of Goods should apply or be excluded if the contract involves international parties.

Address the allocation of attorneys' fees and costs in dispute resolution, either requiring each party to bear its own fees and costs, or providing that the prevailing party in any dispute will be entitled to recover its reasonable attorneys' fees and costs from the non-prevailing party.

Include a waiver of jury trial if the parties wish to have any litigation resolved by a judge rather than a jury, which can expedite proceedings and reduce unpredictability.

### Administrative Provisions and Contract Management

Establish notice requirements specifying how all notices, demands, and communications under the contract must be delivered. Require that notices be in writing and delivered by personal delivery, overnight courier, certified mail return receipt requested, or email to specified addresses and recipients for each party. Specify that notices are deemed effective upon receipt or a specified number of days after mailing.

Address assignment and subcontracting restrictions, typically prohibiting the contractor from assigning the contract or subcontracting substantial portions of the work without the owner's prior written consent, while allowing the owner to freely assign the contract to affiliates or lenders. Require the contractor to remain fully responsible for all subcontractor performance even when subcontracting is permitted.

Include standard contract interpretation provisions such as entire agreement clauses confirming that the contract represents the complete agreement between the parties and supersedes all prior negotiations and understandings, amendment provisions requiring that all modifications be in writing and signed by both parties, severability provisions providing that if any provision is found invalid the remainder of the contract remains enforceable, and waiver provisions clarifying that failure to enforce any provision does not constitute a waiver of the right to enforce that provision in the future.

Address confidentiality obligations requiring both parties to maintain the confidentiality of proprietary information, trade secrets, and commercially sensitive information disclosed during the contract, with exceptions for information that is publicly available, independently developed, or required to be disclosed by law.

Include force majeure provisions excusing performance delays or failures caused by events beyond a party's reasonable control such as acts of God, war, terrorism, epidemics, government actions, labor disputes not involving the affected party's employees, or other specified events, provided the affected party provides prompt notice and uses reasonable efforts to mitigate the impact.

## Document Finalization and Quality Review

After drafting all substantive provisions, prepare the signature blocks for execution by authorized representatives of both parties. Include the full legal name of each entity, signature lines for authorized signatories, printed name and title lines, and date lines. If the contract requires notarization, witness signatures, or other formalities under applicable law, include appropriate acknowledgment blocks.

Before presenting the final draft, conduct a comprehensive quality review to ensure internal consistency, verify that all defined terms are used consistently throughout the document, confirm that all cross-references are accurate, check that all exhibits and schedules referenced in the contract are identified and will be attached, and ensure that the contract addresses all material business terms and risk allocation issues appropriate for an EPC contract of this type and magnitude.

If any critical information necessary to complete the contract is not available in the source documents or has not been provided by the user, identify these gaps and request the necessary information before finalizing the contract. Present the completed contract in a clear, professional format suitable for legal review and execution.
